sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The purple square moved from (52-54, 9-11) to (50-52, 11-13) after clicking on the gray dot at (51, 12). So clicking on a gray dot moves the purple square to that dot's position. The purple square is moving along the diagonal path toward the blue object.
Now I need to continue clicking on the remaining gray dots to move the purple square all the way to the blue object. The gray dots are:
- id=21: (49, 14)
- id=20: (47, 16)
- id=19: (45, 18)
- ...
- id=5: (17, 46)
That's about 17 more gray dots to click. Plus I've already used 3 actions. So total would be about 20 actions, which matches the budget of ~22.
Let me batch click all the remaining gray dots in order. The sequence would be:
(49, 14), (47, 16), (45, 18), (43, 20), (41, 22), (39, 24), (37, 26), (35, 28), (33, 30), (31, 32), (29, 34), (27, 36), (25, 38), (23, 40), (21, 42), (19, 44), (17, 46)
That's 17 more clicks. But wait, after the last gray dot at (17, 46), the purple square would be very close to the blue object. Maybe I need to click on the blue object itself or the gray square inside it to complete the level.
Let me batch all the remaining gray dot clicks and see what happens.

# Batch click all remaining gray dots toward the blue object
# Current purple position: (50-52, 11-13)
# Remaining gray dots in order:

    (49, 14),  # id=21
    (47, 16),  # id=20
    (45, 18),  # id=19
    (43, 20),  # id=18
    (41, 22),  # id=17
    (39, 24),  # id=16
    (37, 26),  # id=15
    (35, 28),  # id=14
    (33, 30),  # id=13

    (29, 34),  # id=11
    (27, 36),  # id=10
    (25, 38),  # id=9
    (23, 40),  # id=8
    (21, 42),  # id=7
    (19, 44),  # id=6
    (17, 46),  # id=5
]

actions = [{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': r, 'col': c} for r, c in gray_dots]
result = action(actions)
print("Result:", last_action_result)
print("Level completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
print("Done:", last_action_result.get('done'))

# Check final purple position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'p':
        print(f"Purple id={n['id']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
